description: Figure 1 presents a schematic diagram of the air induction melting process with a flux cover for Fe-Al-based alloys, showing two stages. Panel (a) illustrates the melting configuration: a MgO/Al2O3-lined crucible with an inner diameter of 150 mm and total height of 300 mm contains molten metal to a height of 240 mm, topped by a 2–4 mm-thick molten slag layer, with an induction coil surrounding the crucible and a tundish feeding an 80-mm-wide mould. Panel (b) depicts the pouring stage, where the tilted crucible delivers molten metal and slag through the tundish into the mould to form a solidified ingot. The accompanying text states that this air induction melting with a proprietary flux cover (Al2O3-CaO-MgO-CaF2) and charging schedule is employed to produce sound, porosity-free Fe-7 wt.% Al and Fe-9 wt.% Al ingots by shielding the melt from water vapor, thereby preventing hydrogen pick-up and offering a cost-effective alternative to vacuum induction melting.

Figure 2 presents a line chart plotting the standard Gibbs free energy of formation (ΔF°) as a function of temperature for MgO and Al₂O₃, relevant to crucible lining selection during melting of Fe-Al alloys. The x-axis shows temperature from 0 to 2400 °C and the y-axis shows ΔF° in K.Cals from approximately -300 to -100. Open circles denote MgO and solid circles denote Al₂O₃. Both oxides exhibit increasing ΔF° (becoming less negative) with rising temperature, but with different slopes. The two curves intersect at approximately 1600 °C (around 1873 K), which caption states is near the melting point of iron aluminides. Below this intersection, MgO possesses a more negative free energy, whereas above it Al₂O₃ becomes thermodynamically more stable. This thermodynamic evidence supports the paper's discussion that molten aluminum in Fe-Al alloys can reduce MgO crucible linings at high temperatures, leading to Mg pickup and crucible erosion, thereby guiding the selection of appropriate refractory materials.

Figure 3 presents macroscopic photographs of cold-rolled Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy products fabricated by the air induction melting with flux cover (AIMFC) process. Subfigure (a) displays flat plates and sheet strips of varying lengths and widths, demonstrating the alloy's formability into semi-finished flat products. Subfigure (b) shows four metallic foils of different sizes curled into cylindrical shapes, with a 2 cm scale bar providing size reference, confirming successful reduction to thin foil geometries. The images collectively demonstrate that Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy produced via AIMFC can be extensively cold-rolled into thin sheets and foils, matching the processability of vacuum induction melted (VIM) material. As stated in the caption and supported by the text, these cold-rolled products are derived from sound, porosity-free AIMFC ingots that exhibit excellent hot and cold workability, and the cold-rolled and annealed sheet exhibits excellent room-temperature ductility (approximately 40% elongation), reinforcing the potential of this cost-effective route for producing lightweight Fe-Al steel sheets.

Figure 4 presents optical micrographs of hot-rolled Fe-Al lightweight steels prepared by different melting routes. Panel (a) shows the alloy with 7 wt.% Al produced by air induction melting (AIM), exhibiting coarse equiaxed grains with a visible 250 μm scale bar. Panel (b) shows the 7 wt.% Al alloy made by air induction melting with a proprietary flux cover (AIMFC), while panel (c) shows the AIMFC alloy containing 9 wt.% Al. Panels (d) and (e) display the 7 wt.% Al and 9 wt.% Al alloys, respectively, produced by vacuum induction melting (VIM). All images reveal coarse, equiaxed grain morphologies. The caption identifies these as annealed microstructures, and the accompanying text reports that the grain sizes of AIMFC alloys are comparable to those of VIM alloys. The figure supports the core conclusion that the cost-effective AIMFC route produces microstructural quality similar to VIM, demonstrating viable production of sound, hot-workable Fe-Al alloy plates.

Figure 5 presents optical micrographs showing the microstructure evolution in hot-rolled Fe-7 wt.% Al plates with carbon contents from 0.012 to 1.0 wt.%. Subfigure (a) for 0.012 wt.% C displays coarse equiaxed grains of a single-phase structure with essentially no precipitates. Subfigures (b) 0.15 wt.%, (c) 0.35 wt.%, (d) 0.5 wt.%, and (e) 1.0 wt.% C show progressively increasing amounts of precipitates decorating grain boundaries and dispersed within grains, with (e) exhibiting a high density of phases at the 250 μm scale. The accompanying text reports that these precipitates were identified as Fe3AlC0.5 by X-ray diffraction and that their volume fraction rises significantly with carbon content. This systematic transformation from a disordered α-Fe(Al) solid solution to a two-phase microstructure supports the conclusion that carbon addition strongly influences phase stability and the mechanical property balance in these lightweight steels.

Figure 6 presents two X-ray diffraction (XRD) traces obtained using Cu-Kα radiation, plotting Intensity (A.U) versus 2θ position, to compare phase constitution in Fe-7 wt.% Al alloys with varying carbon content. Subfigure (a) for Fe-7 wt.% Al-0.012 wt.% C shows diffraction peaks indexed solely to Fe3Al—specifically (111), (220), (400), (422), and (440) reflections—indicating a single-phase condition. Subfigure (b) for Fe-7 wt.% Al-0.15 wt.% C exhibits the same Fe3Al peaks together with additional (200) and (311) reflections attributed to Fe3AlC0.5, confirming precipitation of the carbide phase. The caption states that similar Fe3AlC0.5 peaks were observed in three companion alloys containing 0.35–1 wt.% carbon. Citation text reports that the volume fraction of these precipitates increases significantly with carbon content. Thus, the figure provides direct diffraction evidence linking elevated carbon levels to the formation and increasing prevalence of Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates in lightweight Fe-Al-C steels.
